title: Life cycle assessment and waste
examTip: LCA (life cycle assessment) is a favourite 6-mark evaluation question. Know the four stages (extraction → manufacture → use → disposal), what is assessed at each stage, and how to compare two products. Also understand what 'reduce, reuse, recycle' means in the context of limited resources and why each step is preferred over the next.
introTips: LCA looks at a product's entire environmental impact from raw material to disposal — not just manufacture or use.
When comparing two products using LCA, you need to consider ALL four stages, not just the most obvious one.
AQA sometimes gives LCA data and asks you to draw conclusions — be critical, as LCA data can be subjective (how you weight different impacts).
intro: Is a paper bag really better for the environment than a plastic one? The answer depends on what you count — making paper uses a lot of water and energy, while plastic may last longer per use. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the systematic way of comparing products across their whole life, from the moment raw materials are extracted to final disposal. It's a powerful tool, but not a perfect one.

keyPoints.heading: The four stages of a life cycle assessment
keyPoints.content: A life cycle assessment (LCA) considers the environmental impact of a product across its entire life. The four stages are: (1) Extraction and processing of raw materials — mining, quarrying, harvesting; energy used, habitat destruction, waste produced. (2) Manufacturing and packaging — energy used in production, chemicals involved, waste and emissions from factories. (3) Use — energy consumed during use, pollution produced during use (e.g. car exhaust), how long the product lasts. (4) Disposal — what happens at end of life: landfill, incineration, recycling, or biodegradation. Each stage contributes to the total environmental impact.
writeBullets: LCA: systematic assessment of environmental impact across a product's whole life
Stage 1 — Extraction of raw materials: energy, habitat destruction, waste
Stage 2 — Manufacture and packaging: energy, factory emissions, water use
Stage 3 — Use: energy consumed, pollution produced, product lifetime
Stage 4 — Disposal: landfill, incineration, recycling, or biodegradation

keyTerms.def: A systematic method for evaluating the total environmental impact of a product across all four stages of its life: extraction, manufacture, use, and disposal
The total amount of CO₂ (and equivalent greenhouse gases) produced through all stages of a product's or activity's life cycle

keyPoints.memTip: Four LCA stages: EMUD — Extraction, Manufacture, Use, Disposal. At each stage, think: What energy is used? What pollution is produced? What resources are consumed? What waste results?

keyPoints.heading: Comparing products using LCA
keyPoints.content: LCA allows comparison of different products for the same purpose. Example: comparing a paper bag vs a plastic bag. Paper bag: extraction (tree felling, water and energy for pulping), manufacture (energy, water, chemicals), use (sturdy, can be reused a few times, biodegrades), disposal (biodegrades → low long-term impact). Plastic bag: extraction (crude oil refining), manufacture (lower energy than paper), use (lightweight, can be reused many times if strong), disposal (doesn't biodegrade → persists in landfill and environment for hundreds of years). Conclusion depends on weighting — if reuse is high, plastic may have lower impact per use; if disposal is prioritised, paper wins. LCA is not always straightforward — some impacts are difficult to quantify.
writeBullets: LCA compares total environmental impact across all four stages
Paper bag: high energy/water in manufacture, but biodegrades
Plastic bag: lower energy to manufacture, but persists in environment for hundreds of years
Result depends on what you prioritise: reuse, disposal, or manufacture impact
LCA is not perfectly objective — some impacts are hard to quantify; data can be selective

keyTerms.def: A material that can be broken down by microorganisms into simpler substances — biodegradable waste causes less long-term environmental damage
A material that cannot be broken down by microorganisms — e.g. most plastics; accumulates in the environment

keyPoints.memTip: When comparing with LCA: don't just look at one stage. A paper bag uses more energy to make, but biodegrades. A plastic bag uses less energy to make, but persists for centuries. Which is 'better' depends on how you weigh the stages — and AQA will ask you to evaluate both.

keyPoints.heading: Reduce, reuse, recycle — the waste hierarchy
keyPoints.content: To reduce the environmental impact of finite resource use and waste, the waste hierarchy (or '3 Rs') is used — in order of preference: (1) Reduce: use fewer resources in the first place — the most effective approach. (2) Reuse: use items multiple times before disposal — extends product lifetime. (3) Recycle: process used materials to make new products — less effective than reduce or reuse, but better than disposal. Recycling metals saves significant energy compared to extracting from ore (aluminium recycling uses ~5% of the energy of primary smelting). Recycling also reduces landfill, pollution, and the need for new mining or extraction.
writeBullets: Waste hierarchy: Reduce > Reuse > Recycle (in order of environmental preference)
Reduce: use fewer resources — most effective, avoids waste entirely
Reuse: use items multiple times — extends life before disposal
Recycle: process waste into new products — better than landfill
Aluminium recycling uses ~5% of the energy of primary smelting from ore
Recycling reduces landfill, pollution, and demand for finite resources

keyTerms.def: The order of preference for dealing with waste: reduce → reuse → recycle → recovery → disposal (landfill being last resort)
Collecting and reprocessing used materials into new products, reducing the demand for raw material extraction

keyPoints.memTip: Reduce > Reuse > Recycle — in that order. Reducing is best because you don't create waste in the first place. Recycling is good but uses energy. The key LCA benefit of recycling metals: it saves far more energy than mining new ore.

tables.title: Life cycle assessment — four stages
rows: Energy use, habitat destruction, pollution from extraction
Energy consumed, water use, emissions, waste from factories
How product is used over its lifetime
Energy consumed in use, pollution produced, product durability (longer = better)
Landfill (land use, toxic leachate), incineration (CO₂), recycling (resource recovery)

0.question: Which of the following correctly lists all four stages of a life cycle assessment?
options: Extraction of raw materials → manufacture → use → disposal
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — the four stages are: extraction of raw materials, manufacture (and packaging), use, and disposal. All four together constitute a full LCA.
0.wrongFeedback: A full LCA covers: (1) extraction of raw materials, (2) manufacture and packaging, (3) use, (4) disposal. Transport is sometimes included within manufacture or use stages. Recycling is one possible disposal method, not a separate stage.

1.question: Why is 'reduce' preferred over 'recycle' in the waste hierarchy?
options: Recycling is more expensive than reducing resource use
Reducing resource use prevents waste from being created in the first place, which is more effective than processing waste after it's created
Recycling always produces lower quality materials than the original
Reducing is only applicable to plastic, not to metals or paper
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — reducing means not creating the waste or resource use in the first place. Recycling is better than landfill but still uses energy and resources for collection and processing.
1.wrongFeedback: Reducing is preferred because it eliminates the problem before it occurs — no waste is created, no resources are used unnecessarily. Recycling is beneficial but still requires energy for collection, sorting, and reprocessing. Reduction avoids these costs entirely.

commonMistakes: Only considering one stage of an LCA when comparing products — you must assess all four stages. A product that's cheap to make might be very polluting to dispose of.

Saying LCA gives a definitive 'correct' answer — it doesn't. Different LCA analyses may weight stages differently, making comparisons subjective. AQA may ask you to identify limitations of LCA for this reason.

Saying recycling has no environmental cost — recycling saves resources and energy compared to extraction, but it still uses energy for collection, sorting, and processing. It's better than landfill, not zero-impact.

Confusing reuse with recycling — reuse means using the same item again (e.g. using a glass bottle multiple times). Recycling means processing materials into something new (e.g. melting glass to make new bottles).
examTips: For LCA comparison questions, structure your answer by going through all four stages for each product, then drawing a conclusion. Don't just compare the most obvious stage.

When asked about limitations of LCA: data collection is difficult and expensive; different analysts may weight stages differently; some impacts (e.g. habitat destruction) are hard to quantify numerically.
For 'reduce, reuse, recycle' questions, always state the order and explain why reduce is most preferred — it eliminates waste creation entirely.
Recycling metal question: state the energy saving (aluminium recycling uses ~5% of energy of primary smelting). This is a strong factual point for extended answer marks.
revisionCardBullets: LCA = life cycle assessment — evaluates total environmental impact of a product
Four stages: Extraction → Manufacture → Use → Disposal
Each stage: consider energy used, pollution produced, resources consumed
LCA is not perfectly objective — some impacts hard to quantify; data can be selective
Waste hierarchy: Reduce > Reuse > Recycle (in order of preference)
Reduce: eliminate waste before creation — most effective
Reuse: extend product life by using multiple times
Recycle: process waste into new materials — better than landfill
Aluminium recycling: uses ~5% of energy of smelting from ore
Biodegradable = breaks down naturally; non-biodegradable (most plastics) = persists in environment
